turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 17-7505, Madison v. Alabama.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The Eighth Amendment span on cruel and unusual punishments prevents a state from executing the prisoner who is mentally incompetent, but what exactly does that mean? Two of our prior cases, Ford and Panetti dealt with prisoners who suffered from pervasive delusions associated with schizophrenia and psychosis. In those two cases, we set out a test for mental incompetency. A person cannot be executed, we said, if he has a mental disorder that makes him unable to rationally understand the reasons for his punishment, or said just a bit differently, he cannot be executed if he's unable to grasp the execution's meaning and purpose or the link between his crime and its punishment. The prisoner in today's case suffers from a different kind of mental disorder than the one this Court confronted in Ford and Panetti. Several decades ago, Vernon Madison killed a police officer and was sentenced to death. But more recently, he had two debilitating strokes and was diagnosed with vascular dementia. That condition has caused disorientation and confusion, cognitive impairment and memory loss. Among other things, Madison says he could no longer recollect the crime for which he has been sentenced to die. Madison asked the Alabama courts to stay his execution on the ground that he was mentally incompetent to suffer that punishment under Ford and Panetti. After the state court refused Madison's request, we stayed the execution ourselves and agreed to hear his case. The case raises two distinct questions. The first is whether the Eighth Amendment prohibits executing a prisoner whenever he cannot remember committing his crime. We hold that such a lack of memory is not alone enough to spare a prisoner from execution. A person who has forgotten his crime may still have a rational understanding of why the state has decided to punish him for it, and when that is true, his execution may proceed under Ford and Panetti. The second question is whether the execution can proceed if Madison shows that beyond not remembering his crime, he cannot rationally understand the reasons for his punishment. Would it then matter that the cause of that inability is dementia rather than psychotic delusions? We hold that it would not. The Eighth Amendment does not care about the particular diagnosis; schizophrenia, dementia or something else entirely. If a person suffering from any mental disorder, dementia included, is unable to rationally understand why the state wants to execute him then the Eighth Amendment does not allow the execution. Ford and Panetti dealt with schizophrenic prisoners but everything those decisions say applies in exactly the same way to those like Madison, who have dementia. The final issue is what all this means for Vernon Madison? Based on our review of the record, we cannot be sure that the state court recognized that Madison's dementia might render him incompetent to be executed. Rather, there is some reason to think that the state court accepted Alabama's argument that Ford and Panetti extended only to prisoners who suffer from psychotic delusions. Because of that possibility, we vacate the state court's judgment and remand Madison's case for further proceedings in light of our decision. If Alabama is to execute Madison, the Eighth Amendment requires and the state court must find that he understand why. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Thomas and Gorsuch join. Justice Kavanaugh took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
